chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Ergun Akleman
Email: ergun.akleman@tamu.edu
Office: Langford A 105
Phone: 979 739 0094
Office Hours
Flexible

Preferred Contact Method
Request a meeting through phone message or email 
Webpage: people.tamu.edu/~ergun/courses/viza654
Biography
I have been a faculty member at Texas A&M University since 1995, contributing to its
academic and creative communities for nearly three decades. During this time, I have held
positions as a Professor in the Departments of Visualization and Architecture and am
currently a Professor in the Visual Computing and Computational Media Section of the
newly established School of Performance, Visualization, and Fine Arts.
I also hold a joint
appointment with the Department of Computer Science and Engineering, reflecting the
transdisciplinary nature of my teaching and research.
I received my Ph.D. in Electrical and Computer Engineering from the Georgia Institute of
Technology in 1992, where my academic foundation in engineering laid the groundwork for
my diverse research interests, which span computer graphics, geometric modeling, and
topology. My work focuses on developing innovative computational tools and methodologies
that blend artistic creativity with technical rigor, such as my contributions to topological mesh
modeling and the creation of the TopMod system, widely used by artists and educators.
Beyond academia, I am a professional cartoonist, illustrator, and caricaturist, having
published over 750 works in various formats. My artistic endeavors extend to children's
books, popular science articles, and a bi-monthly column in the flagship magazine of the
IEEE Computer Society, IEEE Computer. My unique integration of artistic intuition with
computational techniques has allowed me to mentor nearly 100 graduate students, many of
whom now hold leadership roles in companies such as PIXAR, Disney, DreamWorks, and
Google.
My ongoing goal is to bridge the disciplines of computation and creativity, advancing fields
like interactive storytelling, computational aesthetics, and design visualization while
continuing to inspire and support the next generation of innovators.

chunks.title: Course Description / Special Course Designation
chunks.content:
Tools and techniques for generation, handling and analysis of two dimensional
digital images; image representation and storage; display, media conversion,
painting and drawing; warping; color space operations, enhancement, filtering and
manipulation.

None

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Graduate classification or approval of instructor.
Cross Listing: VIZA 654/CSCE 646.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
Textbook and/or Resource Materials / Additional Course Information
chunks.content:
This material Is: Recommended
The Digital Image by Donald House

Course Materials:
Additional course materials and quizzes will be available on
WebAssign: https://www.webassign.net/. Students are responsible for
enrolling in WebAssign. For enrollment instructions, please
visit https://www.getenrolled.com/?courseKey=tamu36402313 . The class
key for enrollment is tamu 3640 2313 . To access WebAssign, each
student must pay a content subscription fee.
Videos and PowerPoints:
Additional materials, such as videos and PowerPoint presentations, will
be available in the class’s shared Google Drive:
https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1hX61Exa7etquA2r_IClmJBBMvV
Iediny?usp=drive_link

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Grading Breakdown
Quizzes: 56 points
Projects: 36 points
Class Participation: 8 points
The highest possible total is 100 points.
Grading Scale:
A: Grade > 90 points
B: 80 < Grade ≤ 90 points
C: 70 < Grade ≤ 80 points
D: 60 < Grade ≤ 70 points
F: Grade ≤ 60 points
Quizzes:
We will have weekly quizzes, which will not necessarily be equally
weighted. Quizzes will be given and graded using WebAssign. Make
sure to pay attention to the due dates and times. If you miss the
deadline, your quiz will be graded at 50% of the actual score.
Projects:
Students will be responsible for completing three rendering and
shading projects. The projects will be discussed in the class. To be
graded, all required materials should be uploaded to the class website
and designated Google Drive folder on or before the deadline. There
are three required projects, each equally weighted, contributing
approximately 33.3% (12 points out of 36) to the final grade (out of
100). Grading will be based on both quality and process. If you miss
the deadline, your project will be graded at 50% of the actual score.

Class Participation:
Class participation will be determined mainly by attendance. Students
will lose 1.0 point from their class participation grade for each class
they miss, up to a maximum deduction of 8 points. Students who
regularly contribute insightful comments during class discussions may
receive bonus points in class participation.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).
Course Specific Late Work Policy
Quizzes: If you miss the deadline, your quiz will be graded at 50% of the actual
score.
Projects: If you miss the deadline, your project will be graded at 50% of the
actual score.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Weekly Schedule
1. Class Overview
2. Create-Display-Read & Write color images
3. Antializing
4. Simple Vector to Raster Conversion
5. Complex Vector to Raster Conversion (Completion of Project 1)
6. Image Manipulations
7. Basic Convolution Filters
8. Filtering with Non-stationary Kernels
9. Transformations and Warping (Completion of Project 2)

10. Compositing 
11. Dithering and Screening
12. 2D Diffuse Shader
13. 2D Reflection, Refraction & Fresnel Shader (Completion of Project 3)
14. Iridescence

chunks.title: Course Summary
chunks.content:
CSCE 646 / VIZA 654 | Digital Image | Fall 2025
Instructor: Ergun Akleman | ergun.akleman@tamu.edu
Meets: MW 10:20AM-11:10AM (LEC), MW 11:11AM-12:00PM (LAB) | ARCC 414
Topics: topological mesh modeling, TopMod system, interactive storytelling, computational aesthetics, design visualization, digital effects, digital images algorithms, 2D Computer Graphics, Create-Display-Read & Write color images, Antializing, Simple Vector to Raster Conversion, Complex Vector to Raster Conversion, Basic Convolution Filters, Non-stationary Kernels, Compositing, Dithering, Screening, 2D Diffuse Shader, 2D Reflection, Refraction, Fresnel Shader, Iridescence, generation, handling, analysis, two dimensional digital images, image representation, storage, display, media conversion, painting, drawing, warping, color space operations, enhancement, filtering, manipulation, systems for storage, transformation, software for working with digital images, creative work, research, Image Manipulations, Transformations
Tools: WebAssign, Google Drive
Prerequisites: Graduate classification or approval of instructor
